item.title: Algebraic K-theory
item.description: Connective and non-connective algebraic K-theory via cospans and the Waldhausen S-construction; Verdier sequences in stable and non-stable settings; the non-connective extension via iterated Calkin categories.

item.title: Presentable Categories
item.description: This note reviews the basic theory of presentable categories. We focus on κ-compact objects, Ind-completions, accessible categories, and their role in organizing large categories via filtered colimits.

item.title: Compactly assembled categories
item.description: Dualizable stable categories from the compactly assembled viewpoint: R-linear categories, dualizability in $\mathsf{Pr}_{\mathrm{st}}^L$, compactly exhaustible objects, the Lurie–Clausen characterisation, and the symmetric monoidal structure of $\mathsf{Pr}^L_{\mathrm{ca}}$.

item.title: Presentable n-Categories
item.description: This note introduces the notion of presentable n-categories.

item.title: Basic Concepts on Higher Algebra
item.description: This note introduces algebraic patterns and Segal objects, develops operads over algebraic patterns, and studies $\mathcal{O}$-monoidal categories together with $\mathcal{O}$-algebras in the Cartesian setting.

item.title: Continuous (Efimov) K-theory
item.description: Extending algebraic K-theory from $\mathsf{Cat}^{\mathrm{rex}}$ to $\mathsf{Pr}^L_{\mathrm{ca}}$: the continuous Calkin category, Verdier cofibre sequences in the large setting, Efimov's definition of continuous K-theory, its basic properties, and a sketch of the universal property of $K^{\mathrm{cont}}$.

item.title: Stefanich Rings
item.description: This note discusses the colimit of the sequence \[ \mathsf{CAlg}(1\mathsf{Pr}) \hookrightarrow \mathsf{CAlg}(2\mathsf{Pr}) \hookrightarrow \cdots \hookrightarrow \mathsf{CAlg}(n\mathsf{Pr}) \hookrightarrow \cdots . \] in $1\mathsf{Pr}$, and discuss the $n$-affine, $n$-proper, $n$-suave and $n$-prim maps.

item.title: Everything Becomes Affine Under Sufficient Categorification
item.description: This note constructs the six-functor formalism on \(n\mathsf{Pr}_{(-)}\), shows that it is sheafy with respect to fpqc topology, and then extends the theory from affine schemes of derived rings to stacks.

item.title: Compactly assembled category
item.description: A presentable category that is a retract in Pr^L of a compactly generated one. In the stable setting, equivalent to being dualizable in Pr^L_st.

item.title: Compactly generated category
item.description: A presentable category that is generated under filtered colimits by its compact (i.e. ℵ₀-compact) objects. Equivalently, the Ind-completion of a small idempotent-complete category with finite colimits.

item.title: Conventions
item.description: House conventions for this wiki — what 'category', 'n-category', and 'classical category' mean here, and the suggested entry structure.

item.title: Presentable category
item.description: An (∞,1)-category that admits all small colimits and is generated under colimits by an essentially small set of κ-compact objects for some regular cardinal κ.

item.title: Stable category
item.description: A category in which the suspension functor is an equivalence and finite limits coincide with finite colimits — the natural replacement for triangulated categories.

item.title: A brief introduction to 6-functor formalisms
item.description: A coherent pass through six-functor formalisms. From the structural properties one wants of cohomology, through the Liu–Zheng / Mann / Scholze span-category packaging and the Cnossen–Lenz–Linskens universal property, to Scholze's organising observation: Poincaré duality for a morphism is *precisely* a dualizability statement in the $2$-category of kernels. We close with Heyer–Mann's suave/prim weakening, Aoki's one-step étale/proper picture, and transmutation to Gestalten.

item.title: Playbook
item.description: Principles, allocation bands, and execution rules for my long-term investing.

item.title: Sheaf Cohomology as Sheafification
item.description: Sheaf cohomology is evaluation of the sheafification: \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \coloneqq (L\mathcal{F})(X)\). Reading Mayer–Vietoris, derived Čech, pushforward, base change and the vanishing \(H^i(\mathrm{Spec}\,R, \widetilde{M}) = 0\) directly off the sheaf condition, without spectral sequences or injective resolutions.

item.title: Synthetic category theory and type theory
item.description:
This page aims to explain how type theory can be understood within the framework of synthetic category theory.

The content of this page is derived from my questions to Tashi during the second exercise class and Tashi’s responses. I would like to express my gratitude to Tashi here.

We focus on the following two questions:

  • Question I. How should we understand the notion of isofibration (hereafter referred to as a fibration) in synthetic category theory?
  • Question II. Do we still have a weak factorization system in this context?

Next, we will answer these questions through the lens of type theory and the categorical perspective of synthetic category theory.

item.title: Stable Dold–Kan and Descent
item.description: Unified exposition of the stable Dold–Kan correspondence and its application to descent theory in stable categories.

item.title: About me
item.description:
Ou Liu (刘欧)

I am a master’s student in Mathematics at the University of Regensburg, with interests in motivic homotopy theory, derived algebraic geometry, and higher category theory. Before Regensburg, I studied at Shanxi University, where my undergraduate thesis applied the six-functor formalism to smooth representation theory.

Personal

  • Name. Ou Liu (刘欧)
  • Date of birth. 2 February 2004 (as commonly observed; the national ID card records 12 January 2004)
  • Age. 22
  • Nationality. Chinese
  • Languages. Chinese (native), English (working), German (elementary, improving)

Education

2025 – Present · M.Sc. in Mathematics
University of Regensburg, Germany.
Research focus on motivic homotopy theory, derived algebraic geometry and higher category theory.

item.title: Graph view
item.description: A force-directed graph of all wiki entries and their cross-references.
